coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots intercalated in polypyrrole (PPy) thin electrodes for accelerated energy storage
coredata.dc:creator: Malik, Rinki
Lata, Suman
Soni, Udit
Rani, Priyanka
Malik, Rajender Singh
coredata.dc:description: Polypyrrole (PPy) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) based composite electrodes (PPy/CQDs or PQDs) are designed through one step electro-polymerization technique in aqueous solution for energy storage purposes. Composite electrode, thus obtained exhibits good electrochemical properties due to dotted CQDs structure comprising of high surface area with numerous active sites. Electrical conductivity of PPy was improved noticeably after modifying with CQDs. The charge transfer resistance (Rp) diminishes from 2.7 Ω to 0.11 Ω for PQD composite due to fast charge diffusion. The impact of varied concentration of CQDs was also evaluated for optimizing the electrochemical behavior of PQDs composites and PPy: CQDs as 95: 5 w/w ratio designated as PQD 0.5 was found to be the most effective composition. Various electrochemical properties of PQDs are also correlated at different time intervals to get an effective electro-polymerized output and observed that PQD 0.5 composition processed for 30 min (PQD 30) demonstrates high specific capacitance of 1073.5 F/g at a scan rate of 10 mV/s and mass loading with 1.4 g/cm2 of the optimized composite with a good cyclic stability as 62% even after 2000 cycles of charge/discharge for the electrode.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polypyrrole
Carbon quantum dots
Energy storage
Electro polymerization
Cyclic voltammetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of Ascorbic acid, PSS and CQDs, (b) UV–Vis spectra of AA+PSS and CQDs (c) XRD pattern of CQDs, (d) TEM images of CQDs, (e) Frequency% Histogram of CQDs, (f) PL spectra of CQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of PPy, CQDs and PQD 0.5 (b) XRD of PQD 0.5, and (c1, c2 and c3) SEM images of CQDs, PPy and PQD 0.5 respectively.
(a) Linear sweep voltammetry scans at 1 mV/s (b) Chronopotentiometric curves.

Electrochemical characteristics of PPy, PQD 0.2, PQD 0.3, PQD 0.4, PQD 0.5 and PQD 0.6 composite (a) CV at scan rate of 10 mV/s (b) GCD at 1 A/cm2 (c) Nyquist plot (d) Admittance plot (e) Circuit diagram for PQD 0.5.
Electrochemical characteristics of composites PQD 10, PQD 20, PQD 30 and PQD 40 (a) Chronoamperometric curves (b) CV (c) GCD (d) Nyquist graph (e) Admittance plot.

(a) CV of PQD 30 at scan rate of 10 mV/s, 20 mV/s, 30 mV/s, 50 mV/s and 100 mV/s scan rate (b) GCD of PQD 30 at 0.001A/cm2, 0.002 A/cm2, 0.003A/cm2,0.004A/cm2, 0.005A/cm2, 0.01 A/cm2current density (c) Coulombic efficiency and specific capacitance at different current density of PQD 30.
(a) Ragone Plot of PQD 30 (b) Cycling stability of PQD 30.
Details of composite prepared along with their Designation.
Details of composite at different polymerization time along with sample designation.
Mass loading and capacitance of different composites.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fitted data of electrical impedance spectroscopy for different composites.
Mass loading and capacitance of composites at different polymerization time.

row.entry: Areal capacitance (F/cm2) at scan rate 10 mV/s

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots intercalated in polypyrrole (PPy) thin electrodes for accelerated energy storage

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Deenbandhu Chhotu Ram University of Science and Technology, Murthal, Haryana 131039, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Deenbandhu Chhotu Ram University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDeenbandhu Chhotu Ram University of Science and Technology, Murthal, Haryana 131039, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pt. Neki Ram Sharma Government College Rohtak, Haryana 124001, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pt. Neki Ram Sharma Government College Rohtak

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bPt. Neki Ram Sharma Government College Rohtak, Haryana 124001, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Teri School of Advanced studies, Vasant Kunj, New Delhi, Delhi 110070, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Teri School of Advanced studies, Vasant Kunj

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cTeri School of Advanced studies, Vasant Kunj, New Delhi, Delhi 110070, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polypyrrole (PPy) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) based composite electrodes (PPy/CQDs or PQDs) are designed through one step electro-polymerization technique in aqueous solution for energy storage purposes. Composite electrode, thus obtained exhibits good electrochemical properties due to dotted CQDs structure comprising of high surface area with numerous active sites. Electrical conductivity of PPy was improved noticeably after modifying with CQDs. The charge transfer resistance (Rp) diminishes from 2.7 Ω to 0.11 Ω for PQD composite due to fast charge diffusion. The impact of varied concentration of CQDs was also evaluated for optimizing the electrochemical behavior of PQDs composites and PPy: CQDs as 95: 5 w/w ratio designated as PQD 0.5 was found to be the most effective composition. Various electrochemical properties of PQDs are also correlated at different time intervals to get an effective electro-polymerized output and observed that PQD 0.5 composition processed for 30 min (PQD 30) demonstrates high specific capacitance of 1073.5 F/g at a scan rate of 10 mV/s and mass loading with 1.4 g/cm2 of the optimized composite with a good cyclic stability as 62% even after 2000 cycles of charge/discharge for the electrode.

Polypyrrole (PPy) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) based composite electrodes (PPy/CQDs or PQDs) are designed through one step electro-polymerization technique in aqueous solution for energy storage purposes. Composite electrode, thus obtained exhibits good electrochemical properties due to dotted CQDs structure comprising of high surface area with numerous active sites. Electrical conductivity of PPy was improved noticeably after modifying with CQDs. The charge transfer resistance (Rp) diminishes from 2.7 Ω to 0.11 Ω for PQD composite due to fast charge diffusion. The impact of varied concentration of CQDs was also evaluated for optimizing the electrochemical behavior of PQDs composites and PPy: CQDs as 95: 5 w/w ratio designated as PQD 0.5 was found to be the most fruitful composition. Various electrochemical properties of PQDs are also correlated at different time intervals to get an effective electro-polymerized output and observed that PQD 0.5 composition processed for 30 min (PQD 30) demonstrates high specific capacitance of 1073.5 F/g at a scan rate of 10 mV/s and mass loading with 1.4 g/cm2 of the optimized composite with a valuable cyclic stability as 62% even after 2000 cycles of charge/discharge for the electrode.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Twenty first century has evidenced an unparallel growth of energy sources making use of wind, sun, geothermal, hydrothermal, nuclear and even more. Still, an intensive and uninterrupted demand for renewable energy resources with proper eco-balance has continuously advanced the scientists towards alternate fabrication of good energy appliances [1, 2]. Henceforth, in order to meet such challenges, development of some energy storing devices like batteries, capacitors and supercapacitors in an electrochemical fashion are being practiced. Supercapacitors, among such resources, serve as the energy storing systems that release huge amount of energy at higher rate and also generate power through tremendous constancy in long-term cycles to fulfill this demand [3, 4]. Moreover, this triggered area of research is focused on new fabricated/intercalated materials to design some compact and portable sized, low cost and environmentally benign supercapacitors. Broadly speaking, supercapacitors can be categorized into two classes based on their diverse mechanism of energy storage: (i) EDLC (Electric double layer capacitors) (ii) Pseudocapacitors [5]. EDLC stores energy as a result of non-faradic charge storage procedure that occurs in bulk of the material [6] while in a pseudocapacitor, faradic charge/energy is stored due to fast and reversible redox reactions, that is not considered in case of batteries because of dQ/dV i.e. differential capacitance remains constant throughout the potential window and it is not a characteristic of battery materials [7, 8].

Various conducting polymers like PANI (polyaniline), PPy (polypyrrole) and PTh (polythiophene) as well as their composites are being studied as electrode materials as they can accomplish good storage charge capacity at low cost [9, 10]. Due to good electrical conductivity, environment friendly nature, economic with large tendency to store energy, PPy is regarded as the most competent candidate among a large variety of conducting polymers for the energy storage purposes [11, 12]. PPy is a better stuff for the pseudocapacitive charge storage anode due to the feasibility of dual adulteration by anions as well as cations that take place as positive and negative potentials [13]. However, low mechanical strength and poor cyclic stability are few constraints for neat PPy to be used in energy storage appliances. Subsequently, composites of PPy with carbon based materials like graphene, CNT have been studied in order to address such constraints. Young Chen et al. have prepared PPy/CNT based composite by electrodeposition method and found high capacitive value [14]. Santhosh Paul and his coworkers assembled the PPy/MWCNT composite with a specific capacitance of 165 F/g in 1 M KCl electrolyte [15]. Jileli Liu et al. addressed the electrochemical behavior of PANI/SWCNT and specific capacitance value reached up to 706.7 F/g in 0.5 M H2SO4 [16]. Dacheng Zhang and his group reported PPy/graphene based composite synthesized via in-situ polymerization and found 482 F/g specific capacitance at current density of 0.5 A/g with low attenuation after 1000 cycles [17]. Surajit Konwer and his collaborators synthesized PPy/GO composites and obtained high specific capacitance value (421.4 F/g at 2 mA) than neat PPy (237.2 F/g) [18] whereas Jing Li and colleagues fabricated GO/PPy nanowires as a composite and attained 728 F/g specific capacitance value at 0.5 A/g current density with good cyclic stability (only 7% loss after 1000 cycles) [19]. The aforementioned reports about PPy furnish that this polymer, when fabricated with carbon materials, performs better cyclic stability and high capacitance with good power density. Consequently, it provokes the current electrochemists to explore such type of materials/composites with a large variety as better option to be applied in the field.

Recently, Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) - a zero-dimensional (0-D) carbon-based nanomaterial (size < 10 nm) have attracted scientists in the field of energy storage because of their bio-compatible and dispersive nature with, high surface area, good electrical conductivity and satisfactory chemical stability. [20]. As, low surface area is a major limitation for obtaining better electrostatic charge storage in supercapacitors, CQDs with high surface area can support supercapacitors’ performance due to stronger quantum size effect with light weight, elastic nature that enhance their electrode electrolyte interactive interface for better ion transport. Xuan Jian et al. reported PPy nanowired composite electrode reinforced with carbon quantum dots as a flexible electrode for such applications [21]. Yirong Zhu and his co-workers studied CQD decorated RuO2 network for getting improved performance in the field of energy storage [22].

PPy is generally synthesized either from chemical oxidation method or from electropolymerization technique. In the chemical oxidation method, binder like polytertraflouroethylene (PTFE) is required to assemble electrode from powder sample which creates some resistance in the electrode and decreases its specific capacitance [23, 24]. Electropolymerization technique, however, is more suitable method as PPy film directly and uniformly grows over conducting substrate using PPy in nanostructured morphology with appropriate quantity in a single step process [25, 26]. Electropolymerization includes potentiodyanamic, galvanostatic and potentiostatic techniques [27].

In the present work, PPy/CQDs based composite electrodes are fabricated over graphite sheet through controllable galvanostatic electropolymerization technique. This process assists in controlling polymer thickness while current density affects the morphology and conductivity of the film [28]. In this particular procedure, invariable current is applied at a steady rate to get a conducting polymer film. In this method, pyrrole monomeric radical cation (Py) +. is polymerized over graphite electrode to develop porous PPy film. Different electrochemical properties are studied including cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge discharge (GCD) and electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) for supercapacitor application. PPy/CQDs based composite demonstrated outstanding electrochemical performance with significant specific capacitance comparative to the neat PPy that indicates its potency to be used in energy storage appliances.

Pyrrole was obtained from Acros organics, Potassium chloride, Oxalic acid and Ascorbic acid were purchased from Merck Ltd. India, Polystyrene sulphonic acid was supplied by Alfa Aesar and Graphite sheet was supplied by Nickunj Eximp Entp Pvt. Ltd. India. All the reagents were of analytical grade. Pyrrole was distilled prior to use. All other reagents were used as such without further purification. Double distilled water is used in the entire experimental process.

Ascorbic acid (AA) and polystyrene sulphonic acid (PSS) in 2:1 were dissolved in double distilled water and evaporated the solution at 100 °C and thus, a semi-dry solid was obtained. Subsequently, this product was kept in vacuum oven to remove moisture, followed by pyrolysis for 2 h at 180 °C in a tubular furnace. Finally, a blackish-brown powder was obtained that was utilized during further experiments [29].

First of all, graphite sheet of size 1 × 1 cm2 was polished with emery paper of 1500 grade, then ultrasonicated for 30 min to remove the impurities, thereafter, the sheet was rinsed with distilled water and then acetone to eradicate remaining impurities and dried at 70 °C in oven. CQDs were ultrasonicated in 0.01 M oxalic acid for 30 min and then added 0.05 M aqueous solution of pyrrole into this mix and dispersed for another 1 h under ultrasonic waves to get homogenized CQDs with pyrrole, afterward, electropolymerisation was processed at 0–5 °C in galvanostatic mode with 10 mA current range, where reference electrode Ag/AgCl . Subsequent to the electropolymerisation of PPy/CQDs composite at graphite sheet, electrode was rinsed with distilled water multiple times. To study the effect of concentration of CQDs on PPy as PPy/CQDs (PQD) composite, different electrodes were prepared and designated as PQD 0.2, PQD 0.3, PQD 0.4, PQD 0.5 and PQD 0.6 with varied composition of PPy and CQD, shown in Table1.1 . Similarly, to investigate the influence of different time intervals of electropolymerisation on the optimized composition as PQD 0.5, different electropolymerisation timings (in minutes) were applied and the prepared electrodes were labeled as PQD 10, PQD 20, PQD 30 and PQD 40 placed in Table 1.2 . Pure PPy was also synthesized at similar conditions to correlate the data.

Structural information was obtained from Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) in presence of Perkin Elmer Frontier instrument in the frequency range from 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1 with KBr as a reference. The XRD (X-ray diffraction) studies were done through Rigaku Ultima IV automatic high resolution moderator type using Cu K α-radiation at a scanning rate of 1° 2 θ/min. UV–Vis absorption spectra was taken using Shimadzu UV-2450 at wavelength range from 200 – 800 nm. Transmission electron microscopy was performed by Tecnai G 20 (FEI), while Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) was processed with the help of FE-SEM Quanta 200 FEG instrument to characterize surface morphology of samples. Electrochemical measurements were carried out using Electrochemical analyser i.e. PGSTAT 204 Autolab, Netherland with FRA 32 M module installed with NOVA 1.11 software equipped with three electrode system where Ag/AgCl (reference electrode), Pt as a counter electrode and graphite sheet loaded with the composite acted as a working electrode. All the electrochemical measurements were performed in 1 M KCl matrix under a controlled current density and potential window. EIS (electrochemical impedance spectroscopy) measurements were carried out at the frequency range of 105 Hz to 0.1 Hz.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 1 (a) consists of FT-IR spectra for CQDs and explains the role of PSS as surface passivating agent on ascorbic acid (AA). For CQDs, the illustrated peaks are at 3456, 2932 and 2858 cm−1 due to OH stretching and CH asymmetric and symmetric stretching respectively. CQDs spectra herein also elucidate finger print region of PSS from 1250 to 750 cm−1 that evidences about the presence of PSS in CQDs. A broad band at 1752 cm−1 is also observed in CQDs spectra due to presence of C = O stretching vibrations of ascorbic acid and band ascribed at 1640 cm−1 due to C = O groups interaction with (-SO3H) sulphonating groups of PSS, while in AA, it appears at 1665cm−1. A maxima observed at 1043 cm−1 may be as a result of symmetric stretching vibration and broad crest at 1182 cm−1 represents asymmetric vibration of the -SO3 group. The absorbance at 832 cm−1 in PSS is because of CH out of plane vibration for para position while in CQDs shifting in maxima at 845cm−1 is monitored. Fig. 1(b) shows UV- Vis spectra of CQDs and raw components (AA and PSS, both in water). UV–Vis of AA + PSS in water shows band at 229 nm which appears in a range for organic compounds while in CQDs, peak at 230 nm is observed due to Π-Π* transition in aromatic SP2 groups while a hump at 259 nm proves the presence of n-Π* transition, and the transition appears because of red shift which suggests the presence of SP2 nano domains and abundant functional groups as well [30]. This behavior explains the existence of many functional groups like C = O, C = C, SO3 − and the benzene ring (skeleton vibrations) in this region that also explains passivate small carbon quantum dots [31] . Fig. 1(c) shows XRD spectra of the prepared carbon quantum dots where one broad diffraction peak is observed for interplanar spacing 4.2 Å which is higher than that of the bulk graphite inter-planar spacing, this finding matches with that of the Zhang et al. work [32]. The broader peaks, here indicate that these nanoparticle dots are very small, the diameter of CQDs can be determined with the help of Scherrer'sequaton D =
0.89 λ
/
β C o s θ
, where D is average crystalline size of CQDs, λ is wavelength of X-ray (λ=0.15405 nm), β is the full width at half maximum of diffraction peak in degree and θ is Bragg's diffraction angle. The Scherrer's equation gives size of CQDs which is 0.78 nm, and hence, provides their quantum size. TEM images shown in Fig. 1(d) explore the morphology and size distribution of the CQDs. TEM images undoubtedly reveal that the passivated CQDs are spherical and possess mono disperse nano units with a narrow size distribution in their diameter. TEM images histogram in Fig. 1(e) demonstrates that maximum CQDs are distributed in the range of 3 to 7 nm with average diameter as 4.7 nm that ascribed its quantum size. Fig. 1(f) describes PL (photo luminescence) spectra of CQDs which assist in investigating the optical properties. CQDs show green color in UV light at 360 nm. Synthesized CQDs are highlighting excitation dependent on PL emission spectral features. As shown in Fig. 1(f) PL spectra is getting red shifted with decreasing emission intensities as the excitation wavelength increases from 400 nm to 450 nm [33].

Fig. 2 (a) indicates FT-IR spectra of neat PPy, CQDs and PQD 0.5 composite which illustrates interaction between components and effect of CQDs on PPy. Maxima at 3456 cm−1 in CQDs and PPy is observed while in PQD 0.5 it attains a broad crest due to interaction flanked by OH groups of CQDs and PPy NH groups of PPy. Peak at 2932 cm−1 and 2858 cm−1 are observed due to the presence of CH2 stretching. Absorption bands at 1043cm−1 and 1013 cm−1 are visualized in CQDs due to the presence of SO3 − groups. Similarly a broad band in PQD 0.5 is observed at 1031cm−1 which describes the presence of SO3 − groups. Peaks at 963 cm−1 and 749 cm−1 explain out of plane bending of =C H bond of PPy ring. Fig. 2(b) expounds XRD of PQD 0.5 which illustrates PQD 0.5 composite deposited on graphite electrode. Tapered intense peak at 26.5° is observed due to the partially ordered polymerization of pyrrole on graphite sheet. Fig. 2 (c1, c2 and c3 illustrate the SEM images of CQDs, PPy and PQD 0.5 composites. Fig. 2(c1) shows CQDs smooth sheet like morphology, PPy explains cauliflower arrangement while PQD 0.5 composite demonstrates rough heterogeneous sheet like framework which reveals its porosity and cahoots between PPy and CQDs.

Fig. 3 (a) shows linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curve of pyrrole, Pyrrole+CQDs solution and CQDs. Pyrrole and CQDs were observed in 0.01 M aqueous oxalic acid solution where oxalic acid acts as a supporting electrolyte in a potential region of 0.0 to 1.4 V. The scan rate during LSV measurement was 1 mV/s (quite low) so that obtained curves could be treated in quasi-steady state [34]. The response current for oxidation of Pyrrole+CQDs is higher than neat pyrrole while no current response peak is observed for CQDs, which signify that oxidation of pyrrole is controlled by electron transfer kinetics of CQDs in polymerization bath. Fig. 3(b) shows chronopotentiometric curves for electropolymerization of pyrrole with and without CQDs on graphite electrode at current density of 10 mA / cm2. It is observed from Fig. 3(b) that potential rises immediately to a positive peak as a result of double layer and nucleation effect, after that it falls to a steady state in Pyrrole and Pyrrole+CQDs layer, here this behavior evidences about the formation of conducting PPy film.
Fig. 4 (a) shows that cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements of PPy and PQD 0.5 nanocomposites are concieved at a constant scan rate of 10 mV/s within +0.5 V to −0.5 V voltage window.

The shape of cyclic voltammogram for PQD 0.5 sample is not rectangular in shape which substantiates the presence of pseudocapacitive charge storage mechanism. Eq. (1) used for measurement of areal capacitance in CV curves is
(1)
C a
∫ I d V
A ×
Δ
V × υ
Where Ca is areal capacitance (F/cm2), I is response current (A), ΔV is potential window (V), υ is scan rate (V/s), A is the surface area of electrode material (cm2)

While specific capacitance value is calculated with Eq. (2)
(2)
C s
∫ I d V
m ×
Δ
V × υ
Here, m is the weight of deposited material on the electrode of 1 × 1 cm2 surface area.

CV curves of the composites are quasi rectangular in appearance comprising of mirror image symmetry with respect to zero current line and hence, manifest their capacitive behavior. Fig. 4(a) signifies that PQD 0.5 hybrid nanocomposite exhibits highest confined area in CV plot in comparison to that of PPy and other composites (PQD 0.2, PQD 0.3, PQD 0.4, and PQD 0.6) which demonstrates its remarkable specific capacitance (). Table 2 indicates active mass loading, areal capacitance (Ca) and specific capacitance (Cs) parameters of different composites at 20 min electropolymerization time and interprets that PQD 0.5 acquires highest value of Ca as well as Cs i.e. 1.189 F/cm2 and 914.6 F/g respectively with scan rate of 10 mV/s while drop of Ca and Cs values was observed for other composites. However, lowered mass loading of the composites was obtained at increasing CQDs dosage in PPy, out of that, 95/5 as (PPy/CQDs), is the lowest mass loading and after that, further mass loading gets increased. It lucidly indicates that there must be a fruitful interaction between PPy and CQDs up to an appropriate level of dose as shown in Table 2. The assessment of capacitance values got through CV plots is also supported well by GCD plots. Fig. 4(b) depicts areal capacitance of distinct composites at 1 mA/cm2 current density. The relation used for areal capacitance calculation from GCD graphs CGCD is as:
(3)
C
G C D
=
I ×
Δ
t
Δ
V
Where I is current density equal to 1 mA/cm2, Δt is discharge time in seconds and ΔV is potential window (V).

It can be easily grasped from Fig. 4(b) that, with escalation in concentration of CQDs, there is hike in charge discharge time up to PQD 0.5 composite and henceforth, loss in charge discharge time is observed because PPy without CQDs frames thicker film on graphite electrode while in presence of CQDs; it forms thin film that may assist in circulation of Cl−in inner layer of composite and fulfills insertion pathway in PQD 0.5 composite. However, after this, reduction in discharge time is detected because composite film may become fragile.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to evaluate resistance and frequency performance of PPy and PQD composites in the frequency range as 105 Hz - 0.01 Hz shown in Fig. 4(c) and displays that PQD 0.5 have semicircle in high frequency region while vertical spike line in low frequency region explain its supercapacitive character. Additionally, PQD 0.5 depicts lowest solution resistance (2.85 Ω) as well as polarization resistance or charge transfer resistance (117 mΩ) as demonstrated in Table 3 , in comparison to that of the other composites. In Fig. 4(d), PQD 0.5 also shows knee frequency at 115 Hz which defines shorter relaxing time where T0 = 1/f0 = 8 mS. It elucidates the fact that the increased amount of CQDs in PPy enhances an ease of access and circulation rates of Cl− in PPy/CQDs composites. Fig. 4(e) represents circuit diagram of PQD 0.5 which illustrates low resistance values where constant phase element (CPE) symbolized as ‘N’ equals to 1 and is an indispensable property of any capacitor like performance of an electrode material.

Furthermore, Table 4 sums up the electrochemical measurement outcomes of PQD 0.5 compositions at different polymerization timings and displayed in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5(a) signifies chronoamperometric curves of PQD 0.5 composition at different polymerization time intervals at current density 10 mA/cm2 where PQD 30 informs the steady state potential value; it reveals about better and uniform electropolymerization of pyrrole (Py). From Fig. 5(b) and Fig. 5(c), it is examined that PQD 0.5 composition at polymerization time 30 min endows with highest surface area in CV curves with maximum discharge time in GCD findings along with the better specific capacitance value than those monitored at other polymerization time along change in mass polymerization (Table 4). These results may occur due to succeeding aspects that with inflation in polymerization time, there is thicker film developed on working electrode that resists the Cl− to enter in the inner layer of composite, hence insertion reaction of electrolyte would not complete, consequently, drop in capacitance is recognized at higher timings. In Fig. 5(d) EIS is analyzed clarifying that, with optimized polymerization time that equals to 30 min, we get minimized Rs (2.85 Ω) and Rp (0.117 Ω) since nano-land mass of PQD 0.5 composition grows while for 40 min, increase in resistance is noticed because of chunky films on graphite electrode. Admittance plot in Fig. 5(e) retrieves that knee frequency for PQD 10, PQD 20, PQD 30 and PQD 40 is 65 Hz, 115 Hz, 152 Hz and 86 Hz respectively and the calculated relaxation time from knee frequency is 15 ms, 8 ms, 6 ms and 10 ms respectively, out of all these, PQD 30 possesses lowest relaxation time that contributes in delivering higher power by the composite electrode.

PQD 30 was also examined at different scan rates as represented in Fig 6 (a). It can be analyzed from Fig 6. (a) that, with rise in scan rates from 10 mV/s to 100 mV/s, there is not any noticeable change monitored in shape of CV plots and even at scan rate of 100 mV/s, there is 65% retention in the specific capacitance value. All these outcomes describe an excellent rate stability of PQD 30 composite. Fig. 6(b) and Fig. 6(c) explain alteration in specific capacitance with enhancement in current density of PQD 30 composite. Also, with an increased current density from 0.001 A/cm2 to 0.01 A/cm2; there is reduction in CGCD value from 1365 F/cm2 at 0.001 A/cm2 to 817 F/cm2 at 0.01 A/cm2, this analyzes that there is 60% gain of CGCD value even at 0.01 A/cm2 that expounds about PQD 30 stabilized current density. Fig. 6(c) signifies coulombic efficiency at different density data. Coulombic efficiency (charge delivered/ charge stored) is 98% at 0.001 A/cm2 and tells that there is no parasitic reaction at the discharge process of supercapacitor electrode. At higher current density (0.002 A/cm2 to 0.01 A/cm2) coulombic efficiency remains 98% to 88%, which elucidates about PQD 30 as a promising material for energy storage utilization.

Fig. 7 (a) demonstrates Ragone plot of PQD 30 that describes the relation between energy density (W/cm2) and power density (W/cm2). It is quite clear here, that, an energy density of 70.22 Wh/cm2 and power density of 3060 W/cm2 at an operating voltage 1 V. To compute the performance of PQD 30 electrode material, response time (energy density/power density) is further calculated. Response time is 86 s to 1500s at different energy density and power density which clearly elucidates pseudocapacitor characteristics of the electrode material. The cycling stability of PQD 30 composite is calculated at current density 0.003 A/cm2 as shown in Fig. 7(b) where, inset picture shows composite cycling time out. Fig. 7(b) demonstrates that PQD 30 exhibits good cycling stability with 62% retention of the primary specific capacitance even after 2000 cycles. Thus, the PQD 30 electrode material figured out in the present study has a remarkable tendency to be utilized in energy storage and wearable appliances.

PPy/CQDs or PQD composites are fabricated through galvanostatic electropolymerization approach using pyrrole and carbon quantum dots, and further tested for energy storage application. An electrochemical assembly of PPy and CQDs fabricates a heterogeneous layer as PQD composite on graphite sheet. PQD 0.5 demonstrates high specific capacitance of 838 F/g at scan rate of 10 mV/s with mass loading 1.3 g/cm2. PQD 0.5 is also explored at different time intervals for electropolymerization and found that PQD 0.5 composition at 30 min (PQD 30) performs better characteristics for storing energy. Thus, the results evidence about an availability of large surface area, short conductive path and active edge center. Response time is also calculated for the composites at different energy density and power density values that clearly elucidates about improved pseudocapacitive characteristics of the PQD 30 electrode material. In this way, the PQD nanocomposites offer good quality electrode material for designing energy storage devices with suitable characteristics.

Rinki Malik: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing- Original draft preparation, Suman Lata: Formal analysis, Supervision. Udit Soni: Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Priyanka Rani: Data curation and investigation, Rajender Singh Malik: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Supervision, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: I declare that authors have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: University Grant Commission (UGC), Delhi provided fellowship for this research to Rinki as JRF (award Letter No. 19-06-2016 (i) EU-V).
